texto: Eddie and Jim both said it was a great thing the Russians were winning because the strongest team should win. Shannon thought the fascist philosophy was a very comfortable one. You simply cheered for the winner, who proved by virtue of winning that he should have won. No analysis, no doubts, no troubling moral questions.
explicacao: Orientação direta de Helen Potrebenko sobre esforço, persistência e resultados. A afirmação “Eddie and Jim both said it was a great thing the Russians were winning because the strongest team…” funciona como lembrete prático ligado a lideranca, fascism, hockey, útil em momentos de hockey e philosophy.

frase_original: Eddie and Jim both said it was a great thing the Russians were winning because the strongest team should win. Shannon thought the fascist philosophy was a very comfortable one. You simply cheered for the winner, who proved by virtue of winning that he should have won. No analysis, no doubts, no troubling moral questions.

texto: I like driving cab. Receptionists, sales clerks, waitresses -- they all have to look pleasant all the time. I can snarl if I want. There ain't too many women who can do that. Maybe garment workers are allowed to snarl at their sewing machines. But women mostly have to look pleasant when they're fucking miserable, and smile when they're angry.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Helen Potrebenko: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “I like driving cab. Receptionists, sales clerks, waitresses -- they all have to look pleasant all…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, women, pleasant pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de trabalho e reflexao.

frase_original: I like driving cab. Receptionists, sales clerks, waitresses -- they all have to look pleasant all the time. I can snarl if I want. There ain't too many women who can do that. Maybe garment workers are allowed to snarl at their sewing machines. But women mostly have to look pleasant when they're fucking miserable, and smile when they're angry.

texto: So everybody lives the way they're told to.The way they have to. Everybody most of the time. Not ever all the time. Hair style, clothing, manner of speech, ideas, cars, houses, and everything. Just like they have to. Even the way we walk. But not all the time. Comes a time when everybody breaks out. And that's the only characteristic of human nature I know of. Not greed, not obedience, not violence, nor any of those things the capitalists would have us believe are intrinsic. Only rebellion.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Helen Potrebenko: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “So everybody lives the way they're told to.The way they have to. Everybody most of the time. Not …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que tempo, capitalism, human-nature pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de human-nature.

Hair style, clothing, manner of speech, ideas, cars, houses, and everything. Just like they have to. Even the way we walk. But not all the time. Comes a time when everybody breaks out. And that's the only characteristic of human nature I know of. Not greed, not obedience, not violence, nor any of those things the capitalists would have us believe are intrinsic. Only rebellion.
